name: Sound Design & Silent UX
definition_origin: Modern sonic branding theory was formalized by Joel Beckerman in 'The Sonic Boom: How Sound Transforms the Way We Think, Feel, and Buy' (HMH, 2014), founder of Made Music Studio (NBC Sports, AT&T, Disney audio identity). Audrey Arbeeny (Audiobrain founder, 2003) codified the sonic-branding strategy framework via her 'Sonic Branding: The Value of Intentional Audio' essays and her work on the Xbox 360 startup, IBM Watson, and the Olympics theme. The reference text on UI sound is Bill Buxton's foundational 'Sound and Image: Resources for the Ear' (1989) plus Mary Hayes' chapter in Brenda Laurel's 'Computers as Theatre' (1991). On the silence side, Mark Weiser & John Seely Brown's 'Designing Calm Technology' (Xerox PARC, 1995) and Amber Case's 'Calm Technology: Principles and Patterns for Non-Intrusive Design' (O'Reilly, 2015) explicitly argue that audio should live in the periphery — Case's principle 6 is 'Technology can communicate, but doesn't need to speak.'
core_mechanism: Sound bypasses cognitive parsing — a 200ms earcon registers in the auditory cortex ~20-40ms before a visual confirmation parses through V1 → fusiform → meaning. This makes audio the fastest channel for pre-attentive feedback (you hear the email send before you see the toast). Sonic logos exploit episodic-memory consolidation: a 1-3s motif (Netflix ta-dum, Intel bong, Slack 'knock brush') paired with a high-arousal moment (app open, message receive) embeds via classical conditioning into the mesolimbic reward loop after ~7-10 repetitions, then triggers anticipatory dopamine on later replays — Beckerman's 'boom moment.' Silence works inversely via the violation-of-expectation effect: in a notification-saturated environment, the absence of sound is the new salience signal, and default-mute respects what Case calls 'cognitive sovereignty' — the user's right to the periphery. The risk profile is asymmetric: a beloved sonic logo creates compounding brand equity over years, whereas a single annoying notification sound costs you the user in seconds (mute toggle is one tap away).
neuroscience_correlate: Auditory cortex (Heschl's gyrus, primary A1, secondary belt regions) processes earcons in ~30-50ms — faster than visual recognition. Amygdala-mediated startle response triggers on sharp transients >85dB; brand sonic logos are deliberately tuned below this threshold and into pleasant-pitch ranges (200-2000Hz fundamental, harmonic series rather than dissonance). Mesolimbic dopamine spike on classically-conditioned brand sound (the Netflix ta-dum after years of pairing). Default mode network deactivation on unexpected loud notifications interrupts task flow (the rationale for default-mute).
empirical_evidence: Beckerman's Made Music Studio cites a 2017 IPSOS / PHMG study showing brands with strategic sonic identity score 76% higher on emotional connection metrics vs. silent counterparts (n≈2,000, US/UK consumers). Mastercard's sonic logo rollout (2019, designed by Matt Pilling) reported a 2.4x increase in brand-recall index in tracking studies post-launch [Mastercard internal, reported via Fast Company 2020]. PHMG / Sentient Decision Science 2020 'Audio Logo Index' found 74% of consumers said a brand's sonic identity influenced their feelings toward it, with positive sonic branding correlated to a 96% lift in brand recall vs. visual-only. Conversely, the 'Sound of Silence' study (Liao et al., Computers in Human Behavior 2022, n=138) found users in silent mode picked up phones MORE often than those in audio-alert mode, especially high-FoMO subjects — i.e., silence amplifies anxious checking, a counter-intuitive cost of default-mute. NN/g audio research (Pernice & Budiu 2018) on voice/audio interfaces found auditory icons (real-world referent sounds) score ~30% better recognition than abstract earcons. Industry baseline: 80%+ of smartphone users in the US report keeping notifications on vibrate-only or silent (Deloitte Mobile Consumer Survey 2023), validating the default-mute design assumption.
measurable_outcome_metric: Sonic-logo recall in unaided brand-recognition surveys (target ≥40% after 6 months of consistent exposure); notification mute-rate (% of users who disable per-app sounds — lower is better, indicates sound is welcome); session-completion delta with vs. without success-sound (A/B); time-to-first-mute (median session count before user disables sounds); brand-emotional-connection NPS lift; for silent UX: session length and re-open frequency without sound prompts (proxy for un-coerced engagement).
engineering_tactics: iOS — AVFoundation: use AVAudioPlayer for short UI cues (<3s) loaded into memory once; for longer or layered content use AVAudioEngine. CRITICAL: configure AVAudioSession.Category — use .ambient (mixes with other audio, respects Ring/Silent switch) for UI feedback, .playback only for media-first apps. Sample rate 44.1kHz, AAC encoding, peak normalize to -3dBFS, RMS around -16 LUFS. Always pre-load: let player = try AVAudioPlayer(contentsOf: url); player.prepareToPlay(). Pair with haptic via the AVAudioPlayer didFinishPlaying delegate or fire simultaneously for tight audio-haptic sync (target <30ms offset). Android — SoundPool for short UI cues (it pre-decodes and supports concurrent playback up to maxStreams), MediaPlayer for longer assets: val sp = SoundPool.Builder().setMaxStreams(4).setAudioAttributes(AudioAttributes.Builder().setUsage(USAGE_ASSISTANCE_SONIFICATION).setContentType(CONTENT_TYPE_SONIFICATION).build()).build(). USAGE_ASSISTANCE_SONIFICATION respects Do-Not-Disturb. React Native: import { Audio } from 'expo-av'; const { sound } = await Audio.Sound.createAsync(require('./assets/success.m4a')); await sound.setVolumeAsync(0.6); await sound.playAsync(). Always export an AudioContext singleton, cap concurrent plays, and add a global settings.soundsEnabled gate read from a Zustand store. SONIC LOGO — keep total length 0.6-1.5s, 3-5 notes max, harmonic interval (perfect fifth, major third) for 'positive,' minor second / tritone for 'caution.' Render at -14 LUFS (Apple recommended UX level), embed -1dBTP true peak. Provide both 44.1kHz/16-bit AAC for production and uncompressed WAV master in repo. NOTIFICATION SOUNDS — ship custom Slack-style 'knock brush' as .caf on iOS (NotificationSoundName) and .ogg/.wav (≤30s) under /res/raw on Android. Use UNNotificationSound(named:) on iOS; setSound(Uri) on Android NotificationChannel. SILENT UX — default new apps to 'silent + haptic-only' and surface an opt-in onboarding step ('Want to hear celebration sounds? Off by default'). Always honor system Ring/Silent switch.
real_world_examples: Slack 'knock brush' default notification (12 sounds total: knock-brush, hummus, drop, ding, etc.) — the 'hummus' sound was famously recorded by former Head of Brand Anna Pickard speaking the British pronunciation of the chickpea dip; Slack inherited its sonic library from its predecessor game Glitch (Stewart Butterfield, Tiny Speck). Apple Mac startup chime (composed by Jim Reekes, 1991, F#-major chord on Korg Wavestation 'Sandman' preset + Oberheim Matrix-6) — disabled by default 2016-2020, re-enabled in macOS Big Sur. Apple Pay confirm sound — synthetic 880Hz pluck synced with checkmark + UINotificationFeedbackGenerator.success (audio-haptic-visual triad). Netflix ta-dum (composed by Lon Bender, 2015) — a single 3.5s sonic logo that defines the platform; played at title-card to anchor anticipation. Intel bong (Walter Werzowa, 1994) — D♭, D♭, G♭, D♭, A♭ chimed pattern — perhaps the most-played sonic logo in history (reportedly every 5 minutes globally at peak). Mastercard sonic logo rollout (2019, Matt Pilling): point-of-sale terminals worldwide play the same 1.3s motif on payment confirmation — operationalized brand recall. Duolingo lesson-complete: 'ta-da' at +0.7s after checkmark; lesson-incorrect: gentle 'wrong' tone, deliberately non-punitive (designed by Sebastian Schmieg / Duolingo audio team). Things 3 (Cultured Code): completely silent except a soft tick on task-complete checkbox, paired with Taptic-Engine selection feedback — explicit Calm-Tech alignment. Headspace: ambient breath-cue chimes during meditation timers, no UI sounds anywhere else. Robinhood: removed celebratory confetti + sounds in 2021 after regulatory & Reddit backlash about gamifying trading — case study in WHEN to be silent.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: native audio APIs (AVFoundation, ExoPlayer, SoundPool) with low-latency playback, system Ring/Silent switch and Focus/DnD modes, NotificationChannel sound customization, audio-haptic synchronization via Core Haptics. Web: Web Audio API + HTMLAudioElement, but autoplay restrictions (Chrome since 2018, Safari since 2017) require user gesture before any sound — silently fails on first page-load notification chimes. No system-wide silent-switch awareness. Service Workers + Push API can deliver notification sounds, but iOS Safari only supports this since iOS 16.4. Web is sonically much weaker — assume sound CAN'T play unless user has interacted at least once in the session.
platform_design_language_alignment: iOS 26 Liquid Glass design language assumes audio-haptic-visual synchronization for signature moments and silence everywhere else — Apple's HIG 'Playing Audio' page explicitly states that when device is in silent mode 'play only the audio that people explicitly initiate, like media playback, alarms, and audio/video messaging.' Material 3 Expressive (Android 16+) introduces motion-physics with implicit audio pairings on Pixels (notification sound design overhauled in 2024). Both platforms align with default-mute-respectful UX. Ship-faster guidance: provide a 1-3 sound minimal palette (success, error, optional ambient), default OFF for non-critical UI, ON for accessibility-essential cues; never override silent switch.
claude_design_application: Always explicitly specify: trigger event, audio asset path, peak/RMS levels, audio-session category, accessibility/silent-switch behavior, haptic pairing. Example prompt: 'Add a success sound to the habit-completion flow in React Native using expo-av. Load /assets/audio/success.m4a (sample rate 44.1kHz, peak -3dBFS, length 600ms, harmonic two-note D5→A5 pluck). Pre-load on screen mount, play at volume 0.6 simultaneously with Haptics.notificationAsync(Success) and the checkmark animation. Wrap in a useSound() hook that reads settings.soundsEnabled from Zustand AND respects iOS silent switch via Audio.setAudioModeAsync({ playsInSilentModeIOS: false, allowsRecordingIOS: false }). Forbid playing UI sounds on every button — only on submit/complete/error/milestone. Default soundsEnabled to false; surface as opt-in toggle in Settings.' Second prompt: 'Generate a sonic-logo asset spec — 1.2s length, F#-major arpeggio (F#5, A#5, C#6, F#6), 60ms attack, 220ms release per note, plucked-string timbre, target -14 LUFS, true peak -1dBTP. Output as both 44.1kHz/16-bit WAV master and AAC.m4a for delivery.'
stitch_application: Stitch is text/visual only — sound is invisible in mockups. Pivot to (a) describing the visual moment the sound accompanies in elaborate prose, (b) labeling AUDIO INTENT for devs to implement separately, (c) annotating the silence regions explicitly so devs know 'no sound here.' Example fragment: 'When the user taps Submit, the button compresses 4% over 120ms (cubic-bezier 0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) and the checkmark draws in 320ms. AUDIO INTENT (not visualized — implement at code time): a 600ms two-note pluck — D5 then A5, separated by 80ms, plucked-string timbre, peak -3dBFS, RMS -16 LUFS, played at volume 0.6, only if user has soundsEnabled and device is not in silent mode. SILENCE INTENT for the rest of the flow: no UI sound on hover, scroll, tab-change, or modal-open — these moments must remain silent.' Second fragment: 'Onboarding sound-preference screen — heading: "We default to silence. Want celebration sounds?" — toggle off by default, sample-play button next to the toggle that previews a 600ms success cue. SILENT BY DEFAULT is the design choice — make it visible.'
ai_codegen_failure_mode: (1) AI generates a UI sound for every button press → cacophony. Counter: 'Sounds only on milestones — submit, complete, error, send. Never on hover, scroll, tab change, or normal navigation.' (2) AI uses default system sound assets (the iOS 'tink' or Android default click) → branding void. Counter: 'Use only the project's custom sonic-palette assets in /assets/audio. Do not call SystemSoundID 1104 or similar.' (3) AI plays audio at volume 1.0 → too loud. Counter: 'Default volume 0.5-0.7. Reserve 1.0 only for alarm/critical errors.' (4) AI doesn't respect silent switch — plays through anyway. Counter: 'On iOS, set Audio.setAudioModeAsync({ playsInSilentModeIOS: false }). Never set to true unless app is media-first.' (5) AI defaults sounds ON — opt-out instead of opt-in. Counter: 'Default settings.soundsEnabled = false. Onboarding asks; no sound until user opts in.' (6) AI uses an MP3 (>40ms decode latency on Android) for short cues. Counter: 'Use AAC (.m4a) for iOS and OGG/WAV ≤30s for Android short cues; load via SoundPool not MediaPlayer.' (7) AI omits haptic-audio sync — plays sound alone. Counter: 'Every UI sound pairs with a haptic AND a visual within 30ms; never sound alone.' (8) AI gates sound on a flag but forgets accessibility — VoiceOver users may need it as an alternative to muted screen-reader announcements. Counter: 'Provide visual + haptic + audio path; check UIAccessibility.isVoiceOverRunning before suppressing.' (9) AI generates a 3-second sonic-logo where 1.2s would do. Counter: 'Sonic logo strictly 0.6-1.5s, 3-5 notes max, harmonic interval.'
anti_patterns: Loud sound on app open without an opt-in (a violation of public-space norms). Punitive error sound (low-pitched buzz, dissonant) on user mistake — feels like blame. Looped notification sound until acknowledged (only acceptable for alarms). Audio-only feedback with no visual or haptic peer (fails for deaf users + silent mode). Auto-playing video with audio on web home pages (now blocked anyway by Chrome/Safari). 'Engagement audio' — sounds engineered to lure user back (a Robinhood-style move). Different sonic logos for parts of the same product (dilutes brand recall). Ignoring iOS silent switch by category-misuse (.playback when you should use .ambient). Using stock sound libraries for brand sounds — every freemium app sounds the same. AI-slop variant: every state-change wired to expo-av default click sound.
regulatory_risk: Medium. Notification sound abuse can fall under DSA Art. 25 'manipulative interface design' (especially if used to recapture attention via FOMO). UK Age-Appropriate Design Code: kids' apps must not use punitive audio on failure or 'addictive' celebratory audio engineered for compulsive engagement. WCAG 1.4.2 (Audio Control) requires user-controllable audio if it plays >3s. WCAG 1.2.1 (Audio-only Alternatives) — any meaning conveyed in sound must have a visual/text equivalent. Robinhood's 2021 SEC enforcement on 'gamification' explicitly cited celebration sounds as 'engagement engineering' adjacent to wagering — a precedent for fintech and behavior-change apps. COPPA implication for under-13 apps: do not condition rewards on auditory dopamine cues without parental consent.
pitfalls_caveats: Sound is the most context-sensitive UX channel: same chime is delight at home, embarrassment in a meeting. Cultural variation: Western users associate rising-pitch with 'success,' but Mandarin tonal speakers may parse pitch-up sequences as questioning. Accessibility: deaf and hard-of-hearing users need visual+haptic redundancy; sound-sensitive users (ASD, PTSD, hyperacusis) need a global mute. Performance: pre-loading 20 sound assets adds ~3-8MB to install size; lazy-load non-critical cues. Battery: SoundPool/AVAudioPlayer kept warm consume <0.1% but uncapped concurrent streams can spike. Decoder latency on Android: MP3 has ~40ms decode delay vs. ~10ms for OGG — never use MP3 for time-critical UI cues.
cross_cultural_validity: Major variation. Japanese mobile users default to 'manner mode' (silent) by social norm — Japan was the original default-mute culture, codified ~2000s on commuter trains, and Japanese phones historically shipped with very quiet shutter sounds (legally mandated to deter voyeurism — phone cameras CANNOT be muted in Japan/Korea by regulation). Nordic and German enterprise cultures lean silent. US/Brazil/Mexico/India lean louder, with personalized ringtones still socially acceptable. Tonal-language speakers (Mandarin, Cantonese, Vietnamese) parse pitched sound differently — sonic-logo composers often retest in CN-market focus groups. Arabic markets: avoid sounds that could be mistaken for the adhan (call to prayer). RTL has no audio implications. Color/visual symbolism does not transfer to audio — 'red = danger' has no auditory equivalent except culture-specific siren conventions (US emergency-sweep vs. EU two-tone).
sources: [Made Music Studio — Ten Years of Sonic Boom Q&A](https://www.mademusicstudio.com/blog/ten-years-of-sonic-boom-q-a-with-made-music-studio-founder-joel-beckerman)
[Liao et al. (2022) — Sound of Silence: Does Muting Notifications Reduce Phone Use?](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563222001601)
[Mark Weiser & John Seely Brown — Designing Calm Technology (1995)](https://calmtech.com/papers)
[NYT — Decoding the Iconic Sound of a Slack Message](https://collectors.daysofpunk.com/scope/sound-of-a-slack-message-nyt)
